# e2e-token-fetcher

A simple node module that provides easy-to-use methods for getting authentication cookies and access tokens from IDPs. Features automatic caching and refresh upon expiration.

`e2e-token-fetcher` can retrieve:
- `mod_auth_openidc_session` authentication cookies, both with `NetIQ` (`idp2` only) and `Okta`
- `NetIQ` access tokens

## ⚙️ Install

- First, [configure npm to use artifactory](/doc/ARTIFACTORY.md)
- Then, install `e2e-token-fetcher` as a dependency in your projet:
```
$ npm install --save @rd/e2e-token-fetcher
```

## 🔑 Okta prequisites

To retrieve authentication cookies  with `Okta`, you will need to be able to authenticate against `Okta` using an ipn and password. There is no support for USB token or MFA authentication.

This may only be possible on a test environment (https://sso-uat.renault.com) and may require you to connect from the internal network or through the VPN. This also means that you may have to run your CI jobs on internal CI runners. See the `.gitlab-ci.yml` file of this repo if you need inspiration.

## 📖 Usage

```javascript
import { getAuthenticationCookie } from "@rd/e2e-token-fetcher"; // Works with both NetIq and Okta
import { getNetIQAccessToken } from "@rd/e2e-token-fetcher";     // NetIQ only
```

## 📖 API

### getAuthenticationCookie

```javascript
// Returns an authentication cookie for the application located at appUrl
getOktaAuthenticationCookie({
  username: <ipn>,         // set to an ipn valid for your app
  password: <pwd>,         // set to the chosen ipn's password
  appUrl: <url>            // target application url
}).then((cookie: any) => {
  setCookie(cookie.key, cookie.value);
});
```

#### Example output:
```javascript
{
  key: 'mod_auth_openidc_session',
  value: 'd111aedf-b3d1-40cb-aef8-5795f370f3fc',
  domain: 'app-angular.int.skf.dev.gcp.renault.com',
  path: '/',
  secure: true,
  httpOnly: true,
  hostOnly: true,
  creation: '2021-07-29T21:09:40.495Z',
  lastAccessed: '2021-07-29T21:09:40.497Z',
  sameSite: 'lax'
}
```

### getNetIQAcessToken

```javascript
// Returns a NetIQ access token for the application identified by client_id
getNetIQAcessToken({
  username: <ipn>,         // set to an ipn valid for your app
  password: <pwd>,         // set to the chosen ipn's password
  client_id: <uuid>,       // target application client id
  client_secret: <secret>, // target application client secret
  grant_type: <string>,    // optional, defaults to "password"
  scope: <string>          // optional, defaults to "arca"
}).then((token: any) => {
    callApi(url, token.access_token);
});
```

#### Example output:
```javascript
{
  access_token: 'eyJraWQiOiIxNzY3IiwidHlwIjoiSldUIiwiYWxnIjoiUlMyNTYifQ..FHBUDXnC_Q8CAl9UbSbuEF8cFCXxU1xQNnt58kjNA9O3MgQomYOpF7U3B5KBHdQtfcGoYvyLSJ5N7wgBda7TihSGpR7kYv1mqpR1I3_-fiNc2t4KYYbtSMqWkz6l9b1m7MTQJNrU0PY8h6SFIXIolGiiTo2aJQTjMo5r3r0sSsFB9yZiYzVjHLvNaLriR3ihbD-GyuWYpYD2kMldKypK1iNHhQmqtXGpg0VfwOUu6LpxZsbR_De12vDKd9HuI521r0iAQDTrnaYkc5nJxDoLp1Xmt3PymWQaNj-YoOyZhYoWuVICgsIkoMrtSDQNhYO-gULcynQDOvwHgdzOvMcImA',
  token_type: 'bearer',
  expires_in: 1799,
  scope: 'arca'
}
```

## ⚙️ Build

```
$ npm run build
```

## ⚙️ Test

_Note_: running `e2e-token-fetcher` requires setting environment variables will all necessary credentials and URL information. See `Demo` section for a list.

```
$ npm run test
```

You may also only run `NetIQ` tests with:

```
$ npm run test:netiq
```

or only `Okta` tests with:

```
$ npm run test:okta
```

## 📖 Logging

What is logged to the console can be modified by setting the `E2E_TOKEN_FETCHER_LOGLEVEL` environment variable.

| value    | output                                                 |
| -------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `error`  | errors only                                            | 
| `info`   | authentication & cache operations                      |
| `debug`  | proxy configuration, requests, status and redirections |

Default log level is `info`.

## 📖 Caching and automatic refresh

Cookies and tokens will be automatically cached to prevent un-necessary calls to the IDPs.

Access tokens will be automatically refreshed based on their expiration time, while authentication cookies will be refreshed
after 5 minutes as a default, or after `E2E_TOKEN_FETCHER_COOKIE_EXPIRATION` seconds if this environment variable is set.

You may disable caching by adding an extra parameter to API functions calls: `use_cache: false`.

## ⚙️ Proxy configuration

`e2e-token-fetcher` will use the following environment variables to determine which proxy to use to connect to the application and the IDP, in this order:

1. `https_proxy`
2. `HTTPS_PROXY`
3. `http_proxy`
4. `HTTP_PROXY`

Other PROXY environment variables such as `HTTP_PROXY_URL`, `HTTP_PROXY_PORT`... will be ignored.

No proxy will be used for URLs with a hostname present in the `no_proxy` or `NO_PROXY` environment variables (exact match only).

## 🔑 Demo

Demo scripts are provided with `e2e-token-fetcher` to demonstrate the API functions usage.

You may run them as follows, after cloning this repo:
```
e2e-token-fetcher$ npm install
e2e-token-fetcher$ npx ts-node demo/<demo>.ts
```

Please make sure you have set the required environment variables before running any of the demo scripts.

The variables to set depend on the IDP and whether you want to retrieve an authentication cookie or an access token.

- For authentication cookies, depending on whether you want to try `NetIQ` or `Okta`, you will need to set:

```
NETIQ_AUTH_COOKIE_URL=<app url> # Ex: https://cbm.dev.gcp.renault.com/
NETIQ_AUTH_COOKIE_IPN=<ipn>
NETIQ_AUTH_COOKIE_PASSWORD=<password>
```
or:
```
OKTA_AUTH_COOKIE_URL=<app url> # Ex: https://app-angular-skf.ext.gke.dev.gcp.renault.com/
OKTA_AUTH_COOKIE_IPN=<ipn>
OKTA_AUTH_COOKIE_PASSWORD=<password>
```

- For access tokens (`NetIQ` only, using `idp2`), you will need to set:
```
NETIQ_ACCESS_TOKEN_IPN=<ipn>
NETIQ_ACCESS_TOKEN_PASSWORD=<password>
NETIQ_ACCESS_TOKEN_CLIENT_ID=<application client id>
NETIQ_ACCESS_TOKEN_CLIENT_SECRET=<application client secret>
```

Please refer to the demo scripts source code to identify which variable names to use.

You may also set these environment variables in a `.env` file.
